title: Markdown file WYSIWYG display issue

turns.prompt: when I open a markdown file in my repo it is not displayed in WYSIWYG mode

turns.content:
I’m tracing how markdown files are opened and rendered so I can find why the app drops into a plain mode instead of the WYSIWYG editor.

I’m loading the workspace instructions and project identity files first, then I’ll inspect the markdown open flow and the editor selection logic.

I’ve loaded the workspace rules and identity files. Next I’m narrowing this to the file-open path and whichever composable decides whether markdown opens in plain text or the WYSIWYG editor.

I’m searching for the code that opens repository files and hands them to the editor, plus anything that gates markdown-specific behavior on extension or MIME type.

I found prior work that wired file browsing and device auth, so I’m reading the actual screen and navigation code now to see where markdown content stops short of the WYSIWYG composable.

The failure is straightforward now: the editor route always renders a read-only text block. The markdown editor exists, but the active editor screen never uses it, so I’m checking for any existing markdown-file detection helpers before I patch that route.

I confirmed there isn’t any markdown-mode switch in the active app UI. I’m checking whether the app already has a local markdown editor implementation it can use directly, or whether I need a minimal inline fix in the current screen.

The current “markdown editor” in this codebase is still a thin editable text field, but that’s still better than the hardcoded read-only viewer you’re hitting now. I’m checking module wiring so I can either reuse that composable directly or mirror it in the active app screen with the smallest change.

I have the root cause. The active editor screen is hardwired to a read-only `Text`, so markdown never reaches an editable markdown-specific composable. I’m patching that screen now to switch markdown files into an editor view and leave non-markdown files on the existing plain viewer.

```

```

The patch is in. I’m validating the edited screen first for Kotlin errors, then I’ll run a build check so this doesn’t stop at a speculative fix.

The file itself is clean. I’m running a Gradle assemble now to make sure the change still compiles in the real app module and didn’t expose any module-level issues.

The build passed. The bug was exactly where it looked: markdown files were opening in a generic read-only viewer instead of any editor path, and that route is now fixed.

The issue was in the active editor screen, not in the file-loading flow. Markdown files were always routed into a read-only text viewer in

, so they never reached any editable markdown-specific UI.

I changed that screen to detect markdown filenames and render an editable editor surface for .md, .markdown, .mdown, and .mkd files, while leaving non-markdown files on the existing plain viewer. The new markdown branch is in

.

Validation passed. The file has no IDE errors, and Gradle assembleDebug succeeded with no compile failures related to this change.

If you want, the next step is:
1. Install the updated APK on your phone and confirm the open-file behavior now matches what you expect.
2. Replace the temporary editable text field with a richer markdown editor implementation if you want actual toolbar-driven WYSIWYG behavior instead of just opening markdown in edit mode.

toolCalls.name: execution_subagent
toolCalls.summary: Execution complete: Build app to verify markdown editor fix

turns.content:
You picked the richer editor path. I’m inspecting the existing markdown rendering pieces so I can replace the temporary plain text editor with a real markdown editing surface, not just add more placeholders.

I have enough context. I’m replacing the temporary markdown text field with a richer editor that has formatting actions plus rendered preview, and I’ll wire the existing editor route to use it only for markdown files.

```

```

The richer editor component is added. Next I’m swapping the markdown route in the active screen over to this new editor and cleaning out the temporary fallback I added earlier.

```

```

I’ve finished wiring the new editor into the active screen. I’m validating both files now and then I’ll run a full app build, because this change adds AndroidView, Markwon preview, and text-selection helpers.

I found a couple of cleanup leftovers in the new editor helper. I’m trimming those now before the build so the new code stays tight instead of compiling with avoidable noise.

```

```

The cleanup is done. I’m running the real Gradle build now to verify the richer markdown editor compiles end to end in the app module.

The richer markdown editor is in. Markdown files now open into a toolbar-driven editor with Edit, Preview, and Split modes, plus formatting actions for bold, italic, headings, code, lists, quotes, and links. The new editor lives in

, and the active file screen now routes markdown files into it from

.

Preview is rendered with Markwon, so the right pane now shows formatted markdown instead of raw text. The build passed with a clean Gradle assembleDebug run, so the app compiles with the new editor flow.

Natural next steps:
1. Install the updated APK and verify the markdown editing flow on-device.
2. If you want actual save and commit behavior from this editor, I can wire the edited content back into ViewModel state and GitHub update actions next.

toolCalls.name: execution_subagent
toolCalls.summary: Execution complete: Build app after rich markdown editor change

turns.content:
I’m wiring the new editor into state and GitHub save flow now. First I’m reading the current file-content models and API service so I can connect edits to a proper update request instead of bolting on a fake save button.

I found two parallel GitHub service interfaces in the repo, and the active ViewModel is using the app-local one. I’m reading the response and DB pieces tied to that path so I can hook save behavior into the code that actually runs.

The current editor still owns its own text state, so nothing can be saved yet. I’m converting it to a state-driven editor, then I’ll add a real GitHub contents update call with a commit message and a save action in the editor toolbar.

I’m changing the active GitHub service contract first, because the current app-local interface can fetch file contents but it doesn’t expose a correct save request for the contents API.

```

```

I’ve added the save API contract. Next I’m converting the markdown editor from self-contained local state to parent-driven state so edits can actually flow into the ViewModel and be saved.

```

```

I’m wiring file identity, dirty tracking, and save status into the active ViewModel now. That gives the editor screen enough state to show unsaved changes and perform a real commit-backed save.

```

```

I’ve added the save logic to state. Next I’m wiring the editor screen to that state so edits mark the file dirty and the save action can send a commit message through to GitHub.

```

```

I’m updating the editor screen itself now. The goal is one coherent editor flow: dirty state indicator, save button, commit message dialog, and save status/error feedback without leaving the screen.

```

```

The editor route is wired. I’m running targeted error checks now before the full build so I can catch any API/signature mismatches from the ViewModel, navigation, and screen changes.

The edited files are clean. I’m doing the full app build now to verify the new save path compiles through Retrofit, ViewModel, and Compose together.

The build passed, so the save flow is fully wired at compile time. I’m closing out the task state now.

The editor now has real save behavior wired through the active app flow. Edits from the markdown editor propagate into ViewModel state, the file is marked dirty, and the editor top bar exposes a save action that opens a commit-message dialog. Saving sends a proper GitHub contents API update with base64-encoded content plus the current file SHA, then refreshes local file state so the dirty marker clears. The core wiring is in

,

,

, and

.

I also converted the richer markdown editor to be state-driven instead of owning isolated local text, so it can participate in save/dirty tracking cleanly. That piece is in

.

Validation is complete: `.\gradlew.bat clean assembleDebug` succeeded with no errors in the files touched here.

Natural next steps:
1. Install the updated APK and test editing plus saving a markdown file back to GitHub.
2. If you want, I can add autosave to local state and a separate explicit “commit to GitHub” flow instead of using save as the commit action.

toolCalls.name: execution_subagent
toolCalls.summary: Execution complete: Build app after wiring save flow

.

I found the problem area. I’m replacing the reverted editor file wholesale so the behavior is deterministic again: editable WYSIWYG as the default surface, split/preview modes on top, and explicit focus/input handling for the embedded Android editor.
